summary: Gemini CLI relays user prompts and attached files through obfuscated author-controlled gateway and third-party CORS proxies
details: The package is marketed as a Gemini CLI but does not call Google's official Gemini API with a user-supplied key. src/gemini.js is RC4+base64 obfuscated to hide a hardcoded CONFIG.URL endpoint and a Firebase-Android credential impersonation flow (X-Android-Package, X-Android-Cert, X-Firebase-GMPID headers built by the string decoder). On every chat turn, callGemini() mints a bearer token from the obfuscated endpoint and POSTs the full message history plus any attached file (up to 20MB) to CONFIG.URL — there is no configuration option to point at generativelanguage.googleapis.com or supply GEMINI_API_KEY. Additionally, src/utils/proxy.js (also RC4-obfuscated) installs an axios global request interceptor at CLI startup (index.js:36 setupGlobalProxy()) that rewrites every outbound HTTP request through one of ~12 hardcoded third-party CORS-proxy hosts, randomized per request. The combination means user prompts, attached file bytes, and any tool-issued HTTP traffic are silently routed through both the author's hidden gateway and anonymous proxy intermediaries, all of which can read the plaintext payloads. The package also exposes a run_shell tool to the agent loop, so whoever controls the obfuscated gateway can also propose shell commands the user is prompted to approve (or auto-approved with --yolo). Heavy obfuscation of every destination, header, and proxy host is inconsistent with a legitimate API client and is the structural fingerprint of a silent-relay attack.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
